plainMeaning: The Upādhyāya — the monk who teaches the scriptures — is my blessing, my refuge, and the one I praise, and so I let go of my sin.
recitation: uvajjhāyā maṅgalaṁ mujjha, uvajjhāyā mujjha devayā. uvajjhāyā kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ, vosirāmi tti pāvagaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: the Upādhyāya (the preceptor who teaches scripture)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit uvajjhāya < Skt. upādhyāya (upa- 'near' + adhi-√i 'to study/recite'), 'one beside whom one studies'; the fourth Parameṣṭhin.
wordByWord.notes: Spelled ઉવજઝાયા in the book; the same word that names this very sūtra's fourth and final supreme being.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. maṅgala, 'that which is auspicious / removes obstacles'; accusative-predicate singular.
Prakrit mujjha/majjha = Skt. mama/mahyam, first-person possessive/dative.
See first occurrence; the formulaic anaphora repeats the name at each line-head.
wordByWord.notes: Second of three repetitions in the verse — a deliberate liturgical refrain.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit devayā < Skt. devatā, 'divinity'; here the Upādhyāya as the worthy object of one's refuge, not a worldly god.
See first occurrence; third and final naming, governing the kīrtana clause.
Prakrit kittiya, past participle of √kitt (Skt. √kīrt, 'to proclaim/praise'); related to kīrti, 'fame'. Predicated of tāṇaṁ ('their').
wordByWord.notes: Functions as 'their glory is celebrated'; the kīrtana element of the formula.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tāṇaṁ = Skt. teṣāṁ, genitive plural of the demonstrative tad.
wordByWord.notes: Plural — 'the Upādhyāyas' as a class, though named in the singular at line-head.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vosirāmi < Skt. vyutsṛjāmi (vi-ut-√sṛj), 'I let go / abandon'; the technical verb of vyutsarga.
wordByWord.notes: 1st person singular present; the same renunciatory verb used in iriyāvahiyaṁ and kāyotsarga formulae.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tti = Skt. iti, the quotative/closing particle marking the foregoing as a declared resolve.
wordByWord.notes: Seals the refrain: '…thus I cast off sin.'

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pāvaga < Skt. pāpaka, 'evil/sin' (extended -ga form of pāpa); accusative object of vosirāmi.

literalTranslation: The Upādhyāya is my blessing; the Upādhyāya is my deity. The Upādhyāya — their glory is proclaimed; thus I cast off sin.
idiomaticTranslation: The Upādhyāya is my every auspiciousness; the Upādhyāya is the deity of my heart. The Upādhyāya — I proclaim the glory of these preceptors; and so, letting them be my refuge, I renounce my sin.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This closing verse fills the same four-line frame as before, now with the Upādhyāya — the monk-preceptor who imparts the śruta (scriptural learning) to the order. Its placement is pointed: the verse is itself sajjhāy (svādhyāya), and the Upādhyāya is precisely the Parameṣṭhin who makes such study possible. So in the very act of reciting, the practitioner enshrines the figure who transmits what is recited — devotion and discipline fold into one. Where v.3 honoured the Ācārya as the guardian of conduct (cāritra), v.4 completes the living pair of guides by honouring the guardian of knowledge (jñāna/śruta): together they embody the two wings — right knowledge and right conduct — by which the goal of v.1–2 (Arihanta, Siddha) is reached. The triple naming 'uvajjhāyā… uvajjhāyā… uvajjhāyā' is liturgically deliberate: it fixes the mind on a single ideal three times before releasing into the refrain 'vosirāmi tti pāvagaṁ', so that praise (kittiyaṁ) discharges directly into renunciation. Note that this is the fourth and final verse: with the Upādhyāya the printed text closes, leaving the fifth Parameṣṭhin (Sādhu) unsung here — a feature flagged in the preface and notes, not to be silently amended.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain valuation of śruta-jñāna (scriptural knowledge) as a soteriological instrument, not mere erudition: the Upādhyāya's teaching of the Agamas is what lets a soul recognise and shed pāpa. Binding kittiyaṁ (kīrtana, proclamation) to vosirāmi (vyutsarga, casting off) enacts the principle that veneration of the qualified is itself a karma-shedding act — praising the right object reorients the will away from sin. Honouring the teacher of knowledge alongside the teacher of conduct also reflects the ratnatraya logic: samyag-jñāna and samyag-cāritra are inseparable means.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, a practitioner consciously makes the source of true knowledge — and by extension the discipline of study itself — their refuge, then immediately releases sin in the same breath. Practically: let reverence for what you learn translate into the active letting-go of wrongdoing, rather than staying mere admiration.

crossReferences.text: Navkāra (Namaskāra) Mantra — salutation 'namo uvajjhāyāṇaṁ' to the Upādhyāyas
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Ratnatraya — samyag-jñāna and samyag-cāritra as inseparable means

sources.title: Namokar Mantra and the Pañca-Parameṣṭhin (Upādhyāya as fourth supreme being)
sources.consultedFor: Identity and role of the Upādhyāya as teacher of scripture among the five Parameṣṭhins.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra (Gujarati / Hindi / English)
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the Pañca-Parameṣṭhi sajjhāy verse-formula and the Upādhyāya line readings.

sources.title: Tattvārtha-sūtra (Umāsvāti), ch.1 — ratnatraya
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal pairing of right knowledge (jñāna) and right conduct (cāritra) underlying the Upādhyāya/Ācārya distinction.

sources.title: Upādhyāya — definition and etymology (upa-adhi-√i)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology of upādhyāya / Prakrit uvajjhāya as 'one beside whom one studies/recites'.

notes: The four-line formula is identical to the preceding verses with only the Parameṣṭhin's name changing (here uvajjhāyā), so the gloss leans on the established frame. tāṇaṁ (Skt. teṣāṁ, 'of them') is plural while the line-head names the Upādhyāya in the singular — a class-reference, not a textual error. Book orthography is preserved verbatim (ઉવજઝાયા, મુજઝ, the period before the refrain); these are not normalized. pāvagaṁ is the -ga(ka) extension of pāpa, not a distinct lexeme. This is the fourth and final printed verse; the fifth Parameṣṭhin (Sādhu) is not given a separate verse in this edition (see preface) — flagged, not emended.
